seo.title: Healthcare Customer Support AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for customer support AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents in healthcare customer support introduces significant regulatory and patient safety obligations that go far beyond standard enterprise deployments. With HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 governing every interaction, a misconfigured RAG pipeline or unfiltered LLM response can result in PHI exposure, malpractice liability, and fines up to $1.5M per violation. This checklist provides concrete, actionable controls to ensure your AI agent meets compliance requirements before it touches a single patient inquiry.

sections.heading: PHI Protection & Data Handling
sections.description: Ensure all patient health information is identified, de-identified, and contained before reaching any LLM or external service. These controls are non-negotiable under HIPAA and HITECH.

items.label: Deploy a PHI detection layer before LLM ingestion
items.description: Implement a dedicated NER-based PHI scrubber (e.g., AWS Comprehend Medical or equivalent) that intercepts all user inputs and strips 18 HIPAA identifiers — including names, DOBs, MRNs, and phone numbers — before the prompt reaches the LLM. Log all detected PHI occurrences separately in an encrypted audit store.

items.label: Enforce data residency: PHI must not leave the processing environment
items.description: Configure your RAG pipeline and CRM integration to operate within a HIPAA-compliant VPC or on-premises enclave. Disable any LLM API calls that route data to third-party servers not covered by a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA). Validate network egress rules with a quarterly penetration test.

items.label: Sign and maintain BAAs with all third-party vendors
items.description: Before connecting any tool — CRM, ticketing system, knowledge base provider, or LLM API — obtain a signed Business Associate Agreement. Maintain a vendor BAA registry with expiration tracking and annual review cycles. Reject any vendor unable to provide a BAA.

items.label: Implement field-level encryption for CRM data retrieved by the agent
items.description: When the agent calls CRM APIs for order lookup or patient account data, ensure retrieved fields containing PHI are encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256). The agent should only receive the minimum necessary data fields required to answer the query.

items.label: Apply minimum necessary standard to RAG knowledge base retrieval
items.description: Configure retrieval filters so the RAG agent only surfaces documents relevant to the current support context. Prevent retrieval of clinical records, billing histories, or treatment notes unless the support workflow explicitly requires them and the user is authenticated.

items.label: Validate de-identification output with automated test suites
items.description: Run a weekly automated test suite that injects synthetic PHI into the pipeline and verifies the de-identification layer catches 100% of the 18 HIPAA identifiers. Alert on any failure immediately and halt agent traffic until the issue is resolved.

items.label: Restrict agent memory and session persistence to session-scoped tokens only
items.description: Ensure the agent does not persist conversation history containing PHI beyond the active session. Use session-scoped tokens that expire after inactivity and purge in-memory context on session close. Do not store raw conversation logs in any LLM provider's infrastructure.

sections.heading: Auditability & FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance
sections.description: Every AI-assisted decision in a healthcare context must be fully auditable. These controls ensure your agent's actions, retrievals, and escalations are traceable and tamper-evident, satisfying FDA 21 CFR Part 11 and HIPAA audit requirements.

items.label: Implement immutable audit logs for every agent action
items.description: Log every agent action — including tool calls, knowledge base retrievals, CRM queries, ticket creations, and escalation triggers — to a write-once, append-only audit store (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock or equivalent). Each log entry must include a timestamp, session ID, action type, input hash, and output hash.

items.label: Capture full prompt-response pairs with version-pinned model identifiers
items.description: Store the exact prompt sent to the LLM (post-PHI scrubbing), the model version and temperature settings used, and the raw response received. This satisfies FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements for electronic records and enables root-cause analysis when a response is disputed.

items.label: Assign unique trace IDs to every customer support interaction
items.description: Generate a UUID at the start of each support session and propagate it through every downstream system call — CRM, ticketing, RAG retrieval, and LLM API. This enables end-to-end reconstruction of any interaction for compliance investigations or malpractice discovery.

items.label: Enforce role-based access controls on audit log access
items.description: Restrict audit log access to compliance officers and authorized auditors only. Implement MFA for audit log access and log all access attempts to the audit store itself. Conduct quarterly access reviews to remove stale permissions.

items.label: Maintain a model change log with validation records for each deployment
items.description: Document every change to the LLM model version, RAG index, system prompt, or tool configuration in a versioned change log. Each change must include a validation test report showing the agent still meets accuracy and safety benchmarks before promotion to production.

items.label: Implement digital signature verification for automated ticket creation
items.description: When the agent creates support tickets or order lookups via tool calls, apply a cryptographic signature to the action payload to confirm it originated from the authorized agent instance. This satisfies FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature requirements for automated records.

items.label: Retain audit logs for a minimum of 6 years per HIPAA requirements
items.description: Configure your audit log retention policy to retain all interaction records, PHI detection events, and agent decision logs for at least 6 years from the date of creation. Automate lifecycle policies to prevent premature deletion and test restoration procedures annually.

sections.heading: Response Accuracy, Safety & Escalation Controls
sections.description: In healthcare customer support, an inaccurate or inappropriate AI response can directly harm patients. These controls enforce response quality, detect unsafe outputs, and ensure human escalation occurs at the right moments.

items.label: Define and enforce a hard list of topics the agent must never answer
items.description: Maintain an explicit blocklist of topics the agent must refuse and escalate — including clinical diagnoses, medication dosage advice, treatment recommendations, and mental health crisis indicators. Implement a classifier that checks every response against this blocklist before delivery and routes matches to a licensed human agent.

items.label: Implement real-time escalation triggers for patient safety signals
items.description: Configure keyword and semantic classifiers to detect patient safety signals in incoming messages — including expressions of self-harm, medication errors, adverse drug reactions, and emergency symptoms. Any match must immediately pause the agent, alert an on-call human agent, and provide the patient with emergency contact information (e.g., 911, Poison Control).

items.label: Run automated response accuracy evaluations against a healthcare QA benchmark
items.description: Before each production deployment, run the agent against a curated set of 200+ healthcare customer support scenarios with known correct answers. Require a minimum 95% accuracy threshold on policy compliance questions and 100% pass rate on safety-critical scenarios before approving the release.

items.label: Enforce tone and empathy guidelines with an output classifier
items.description: Deploy a secondary classifier that evaluates every agent response for tone consistency — flagging responses that are dismissive, overly clinical, or lack empathy markers appropriate for healthcare contexts. Route flagged responses for human review and use them to fine-tune tone guardrails monthly.

items.label: Implement confidence thresholds for RAG-retrieved answers
items.description: Configure the RAG pipeline to return a retrieval confidence score with each response. If the score falls below a defined threshold (e.g., 0.75 cosine similarity), the agent must acknowledge uncertainty explicitly and offer to connect the patient with a human representative rather than generating a speculative answer.

items.label: Validate that the agent never provides specific insurance coverage determinations
items.description: Add a policy compliance classifier that detects when the agent is about to make a definitive insurance coverage or benefits determination. These determinations must be flagged as requiring human review or redirected to the appropriate benefits team, as incorrect information can cause patients to forgo necessary care.

items.label: Conduct monthly red-team exercises targeting healthcare-specific attack vectors
items.description: Run monthly adversarial testing sessions where internal red-teamers attempt to extract PHI through prompt injection, manipulate the agent into providing clinical advice, or bypass escalation triggers. Document findings and remediate critical vulnerabilities within 5 business days.

items.label: Require human review for all agent responses involving medication or treatment billing
items.description: Flag any support interaction that involves medication order status, treatment billing disputes, or prior authorization inquiries for mandatory human agent review before the response is sent. The AI agent may draft the response but must not send it autonomously in these categories.

sections.heading: Access Control, Infrastructure & SOC 2 Type II Readiness
sections.description: A HIPAA-compliant AI agent requires a hardened infrastructure foundation. These controls address identity management, network security, and the operational practices required to achieve and maintain SOC 2 Type II certification.

items.label: Enforce MFA and least-privilege access for all agent infrastructure components
items.description: Require MFA for all human access to agent infrastructure, including LLM API keys, CRM integrations, RAG index management, and audit log systems. Apply least-privilege IAM policies so each service account has only the permissions required for its specific function. Review and rotate all credentials quarterly.

items.label: Conduct a HIPAA Security Risk Assessment before go-live
items.description: Complete a formal HIPAA Security Risk Assessment (as required by 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(1)) covering the AI agent's data flows, access controls, and threat vectors. Document identified risks, assign risk ratings, and implement mitigations before the agent handles live patient interactions. Repeat annually.

items.label: Implement network segmentation isolating the AI agent from clinical systems
items.description: Deploy the AI agent in a dedicated network segment with explicit firewall rules preventing direct access to EHR systems, clinical databases, or PACS. The agent should only communicate with approved APIs through a controlled API gateway with request validation and rate limiting.

items.label: Configure real-time security monitoring and anomaly detection for agent traffic
items.description: Deploy a SIEM solution that monitors agent API call volumes, unusual retrieval patterns, off-hours access, and repeated PHI detection events. Define alert thresholds for each anomaly type and establish a documented incident response playbook specific to AI agent security events.

items.label: Perform quarterly vulnerability scans and annual penetration tests on agent infrastructure
items.description: Schedule automated vulnerability scans monthly and full penetration tests annually, specifically targeting the RAG pipeline, CRM API integrations, and tool-calling endpoints. Remediate critical findings within 30 days and high findings within 90 days. Provide remediation evidence to your SOC 2 auditor.

items.label: Document and test a data breach response plan specific to AI agent PHI exposure
items.description: Create a written incident response plan covering the scenario where the AI agent exposes PHI — including containment steps, HIPAA breach notification timelines (60 days to HHS, immediate notification to affected individuals), and communication templates. Test the plan with a tabletop exercise every 6 months.

items.label: Establish a formal change management process for all agent updates
items.description: Require that all changes to the agent — including system prompt updates, RAG index refreshes, tool configurations, and model upgrades — go through a documented change management process with a compliance review step. No changes should be deployed to production without sign-off from both engineering and compliance stakeholders.

notes.note: Under HIPAA's Privacy and Security Rules (45 CFR Parts 160 and 164), any AI agent that accesses, processes, or transmits Protected Health Information (PHI) on behalf of a covered entity is a Business Associate and must operate under a signed BAA. The HITECH Act increases civil penalties up to $1.9M per violation category per year and mandates breach notification within 60 days of discovery. Your RAG agent's retrieval logs, CRM query results, and ticket data may all constitute PHI if they contain any of the 18 HIPAA identifiers — treat them accordingly.

If your healthcare organization is FDA-regulated (e.g., pharmaceutical, medical device, or clinical trial support), FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires that all electronic records and electronic signatures used in regulated activities be trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records. For AI agents, this means audit trails must be computer-generated, time-stamped, and tamper-evident; system access must be controlled via unique user IDs; and any automated action that constitutes a regulated record must be attributable to a specific authorized individual or validated system.

SOC 2 Type II certification requires demonstrating that your security controls operate effectively over a minimum 6-month observation period. For AI agent deployments, auditors will specifically examine access control evidence, encryption key management, change management logs, incident response records, and vendor management documentation. Ensure your AI agent's operational controls are instrumented to produce the evidence artifacts your auditor will request — particularly around the availability, confidentiality, and processing integrity trust service criteria.

Beyond federal requirements, healthcare AI deployments must account for state-specific regulations that may impose stricter standards than HIPAA — including California's CMIA (Confidentiality of Medical Information Act), Texas Health & Safety Code Chapter 181, and New York's SHIELD Act. Additionally, if your organization participates in Medicare or Medicaid, CMS Conditions of Participation may impose additional requirements on patient communication systems. Conduct a state-by-state regulatory gap analysis for every geography where the agent will handle patient interactions.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your healthcare AI agent against HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements — catching PHI leaks, policy violations, and unsafe responses before they reach your patients.
